SPY.data_anomaly: fast_info prev_close $666.44 (-0.1%) vs history prev_close $676.33 (-1.5%). Using fast_info as headline; history used for technicals only.

QQQ.data_anomaly: fast_info prev_close $596.81 (+0.1%) vs history prev_close $607.69 (-1.7%). Using fast_info as headline; history used for technicals only.

macro_summary: The February CPI report, released March 12 per the Bureau of Labor Statistics, showed consumer prices rising 0.3% month-over-month — a reading that per CNBC is rapidly fading hopes for near-term Federal Reserve rate cuts, with the 10-year Treasury yield climbing to 4.27% and the US dollar index at 99.7. SPY fell 0.78% and QQQ dropped 0.95% on the session, extending what is now a multi-day technical breakdown below the 100-day moving average; encouragingly, futures are pointing modestly higher pre-market (S&P 500 futures +0.39%, Nasdaq +0.29% per Investing.com and Markets Insider data as of late SGT), and the VIX has pulled back toward 25.18 in overnight trading from yesterday's close of 27.29, suggesting some overnight relief. The dominant geopolitical driver remains the US-Iran conflict and Strait of Hormuz disruption — now entering its second week — which continues to inject both energy price volatility and broader risk-off pressure across the watchlist.
commodities_note: WTI crude shows a data conflict: the pipeline's fast_info source reports $95.76 (+1.34% on the day), while the history-derived close implies a larger prior-session base of $87.25 — the fast_info figure is used as the headline given it reflects the most recent settlement. Note the WTI data anomaly flagged in the pipeline: the 5-day move is a more reliable gauge of trend direction than the single-day percentage. Per Argus Media and the Atlantic Council, Brent crude briefly surged above $120 per barrel last week before retreating toward the $90 range as demand destruction fears — driven by global growth concerns from a prolonged Hormuz blockage — have become the dominant narrative over geopolitical supply fear. Oil Price.com reports Iran has warned crude could hit $200 per barrel, though this is a self-interested threat from a party to the conflict. Gold slipped 0.66% to $5,124 — still at historically elevated levels suggesting ongoing safe-haven demand. No attractive entry into GLD exists at this extension; the commodity-linked action to monitor is XLE, which remains blocked by technical extremes (detailed in the watchlist section).

D05_SI.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at CAUTION. DBS is trading at SGD 55.40, sitting below its declining 50-day average of SGD 57.58 with RSI at 39.6 — neutral-bearish territory — and volume running light, offering no technical floor to build a case for entry. The fundamental headwind is real: per DBS, OCBC, and UOB Q4 2025 earnings releases on SGX, all three Singapore banks saw net interest income decline from banks earning less on loans relative to deposits (net interest margin compression), and the Fed rate pause narrative — reinforced by yesterday's sticky CPI — removes the near-term catalyst that would reverse this. The next key downside reference is the 200-day average at SGD 51.78, roughly 6.5% below current price, which is the structural support level if selling pressure persists. Yield looks attractive at around 5.5% on the data but this alone doesn't justify entry into a declining trend. Not attractive at current levels — would revisit only on a sustained close back above the 50-day average of SGD 57.58 with volume confirmation.

O39_SI.signal_rationale: Signal changed from WATCH to CAUTION — OCBC has slipped below its 50-day average of SGD 20.87 (now at SGD 20.75, -0.58% below) with a declining RSI at 45.1, and the prior WATCH thesis required price to hold near or at the 50-day as a support level, which it has now lost. OCBC had been the relative outperformer among Singapore banks given its YoY profit growth in Q4 2025 (per SGX filings), and per StashAway analysis it led on wealth management fee offsets; but with the 50-day average now acting as resistance rather than support and volume light, the entry gate fails. The 200-day average at SGD 18.04 is the next meaningful structural support — about 13% lower — which is too far away to define a near-term risk level. Not attractive at current levels; would reconsider if price reclaims SGD 20.87 on rising volume, or if the Fed signals rate cuts that could relieve net interest margin pressure.

U11_SI.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at CAUTION. UOB at SGD 36.30 sits 2.5% below its 50-day average of SGD 37.24 — technically just outside the entry zone — with RSI at 42.7 and low volume, consistent with the broader Singapore banking cluster weakness now in its third week. The forward price-to-earnings ratio of 10x is the cheapest in the Singapore bank trio and the 3.9% dividend yield adds income appeal, but the declining earnings trend from net interest margin compression (per Q4 2025 SGX filings) means the multiple isn't obviously cheap on a forward basis. The 200-day average at SGD 35.42, roughly 2.4% below current price, is the next structural support level to watch. No entry until a confirmed close above the 50-day average at SGD 37.24; support at SGD 34.23 is the stop reference if weakness accelerates.

C6L_SI.signal_rationale: Signal changed from WATCH to HOLD — Singapore Airlines has dipped fractionally below its 50-day average of SGD 6.62 (now at SGD 6.59) with RSI at 43.1 and low volume, closing the entry window that made it a WATCH yesterday. The airline is caught in a genuine operational squeeze: per TTGmice (March 12), Asian carriers are cancelling flights and implementing fuel surcharges, with longhaul route surcharges potentially tripling as jet fuel costs surge from the Iran conflict. Per Simple Flying, fuel accounts for 25-35% of airline operating costs, so the current oil shock is a direct hit to margins even as passenger demand holds up. The dual pressure of higher fuel costs and mandatory rerouting around closed Middle Eastern airspace (adding 90-120 minutes per longhaul sector per Threads/aviation sources) makes near-term guidance risk meaningful. Upgrades back to WATCH if the Hormuz situation de-escalates and SIA reclaims SGD 6.62 on improved volume — or if oil pulls back materially below $85/bbl on demand destruction.

Z74_SI.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at WATCH, and entry remains blocked — Singtel is 5.5% above its 50-day average of SGD 4.75 with RSI at 56.4, and the pipeline hard block applies: at 29 consecutive sessions above the 50-day average (one session short of the 30-session minimum for a trend exception) and RSI just above the 55 threshold, neither condition for a sustained trend exception is met. Signal adjusted from the raw WATCH to WATCH (confirmed) — the block holds. The fundamental story is genuinely interesting: Singtel's Digital InfraCo buildout (data centres, subsea connectivity, the RE:AI sovereign AI cloud platform) positions it as Southeast Asia's AI infrastructure backbone, and the 5G expansion provides a recurring revenue base. Resistance at SGD 5.15 is the next ceiling; near-term support is the 100-day average at SGD 4.64 and the sma50 at SGD 4.75. The setup becomes actionable if Singtel pulls back to the SGD 4.75-4.85 zone on any broad market softness or if it trades above SGD 5.15 for 30+ sessions with RSI cooling below 55.

NVDA.signal_rationale: Signal changed from HOLD to CAUTION — NVDA dropped further to $183.14, now 1.3% below its 50-day average of $185.60, and the raw signal has moved to CAUTION from HOLD, reflecting deteriorating near-term momentum with RSI at 48.2 and the stock sitting between two resistance zones at $184.47 and $194.67. The Blackwell Ultra (B300) ramp thesis remains intact per NVDA's Q4 FY2026 earnings call (revenue $68.1B, +73% year-over-year; Q1 FY2027 guidance of $78B), and the $4B reported investment in photonics companies LITE and COHR — per CNBC (March 2) though the specific $2B-per-company split remains unverified pending SEC filings — underscores NVDA's role in building out AI infrastructure supply chains. But the macro environment (sticky CPI, rate pause, mega-cap unwind) is creating valuation pressure on a stock trading at 33x enterprise value-to-EBITDA. Not attractive at current levels — would consider entry only on a pullback to the $177-180 support zone with RSI approaching oversold territory.

AMD.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at CAUTION. AMD fell another 2.3% today to $197.74, now sitting 8.7% below its declining 50-day average of $216.56 — a meaningful distance that reflects genuine trend deterioration, not a temporary dip. The 200-day average at $190.42 is the next structural support level, roughly 3.4% below current price, and that's the level to watch for any stabilization. A reportedly unverified Meta supply deal for up to $100B of GPU procurement (AMD MI450-based, per unverified news reports — not confirmed via SEC filing) and the Ryzen AI 400 desktop launch at MWC 2026 are legitimate longer-term catalysts, but the 100% US tariffs on China and software export bans create direct exposure risk given AMD's China revenue footprint. Not attractive at current levels — revisit if AMD finds support at the 200-day average of $190.42 and RSI drops toward 35-38 (approaching oversold).

INTC.entry_block: CAUTION: forward P/E of 46x (>40x). Requires explicit justification of why earnings growth supports this multiple — default to WATCH unless thesis is clearly articulated with specific catalysts and timeline.
INTC.signal_rationale: Signal changed from WATCH to CAUTION — Intel fell 4.8% today to $45.25, an unusually sharp single-session decline that has reset the technical picture: RSI dropped to 48.7 (neutral-bearish) from what had been a more constructive setup, and the pipeline's soft block on the 46x forward price-to-earnings ratio (above the 40x threshold) now requires explicit earnings growth justification that the 4.8% decline undermines. The CHIPS Act foundry thesis — Intel received up to $7.86 billion in direct funding finalized in November 2024 per Intel's newsroom — and the High-NA extreme ultraviolet lithography ramp (Intel 14A node, first commercial tool installed per ASML's Q4 2025 earnings call) remain the multi-quarter structural drivers. But a one-day 4.8% drop without a clear news catalyst suggests either institutional de-risking or tariff-related supply chain concern flowing through. Earnings are approximately 42 days out (late April). Not attractive at current levels given the sharp drop's uncertain cause; would reconsider if Intel stabilizes above $42.58 support and the 50-day average at $45.84 on recovering volume.

ASML.signal_rationale: Signal changed from WATCH to CAUTION — ASML fell 1.45% to $1,351.58, slipping to 0.86% below its 50-day average of $1,363.32, and the 50-day average itself is rising, which is the one constructive signal remaining. However, the raw signal shifted to CAUTION today and the prior WATCH rationale rested on price holding near the 50-day as a support — that support is now breached. ASML's fundamental thesis remains among the strongest on the watchlist: per TradingView/Invezz, Q4 2025 orders reached €13.2 billion, more than double analyst expectations, and the company is guiding for strong 2026 demand from AI chipmakers. The China tariff escalation (100% US tariffs, software export bans) creates uncertainty around ASML's China-facing order book — China has historically been a significant revenue contributor. Earnings are approximately 33 days out (April 15). Not attractive at current levels; becomes interesting again if it holds the $1,350 area and reclaims the 50-day average on volume — the prior WATCH entry zone was SGD 1,350-1,370, which is now being tested from above.

MU.entry_block: BLOCKED: +5.2% above 50-day SMA (>5% hard block). Sustained trend exception not met: days_above_sma50=4 (<30).
MU.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at WATCH, and entry remains blocked — Micron is 5.2% above its 50-day average of $385.20 with only 4 consecutive sessions above that level, far short of the 30-session minimum required for a trend exception at this extension. The fundamental case is compelling: per market watcher TrendForce (cited by EE News Europe), conventional active-processing memory (DRAM) contract prices are expected to rise 55-60% in Q1 2026 and storage chip (NAND flash) prices up 33-38% — Micron CEO has projected ~40% memory price increases through Q2 2026, though as a seller of memory chips this is an inherently optimistic forecast and should be treated with that context. The high-bandwidth memory expansion for AI data centers (per MU's Q1 FY2026 earnings call) provides the structural demand driver. Critically, Micron reports earnings in approximately 6 days (around March 19) — entering ahead of this binary event carries meaningful risk regardless of the bullish setup. The RSI at 52.1 is neutral. Wait for the earnings print, then reassess if the stock pulls back toward the $363-380 support zone on any post-earnings volatility.

AMZN.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at CAUTION. Amazon at $209.53 is 6.4% below a declining 50-day average of $223.80 — a meaningful downtrend — with RSI at 43.3 and normal volume. AWS remains operationally strong (20% revenue growth in Q3 2025 to $33 billion per CNBC), and the company's own forecast for 2026 capital spending is approximately $200 billion in AI infrastructure (per Q4 2025 earnings call and 10-K), which is the long-term bull case. But that same capex announcement sent shares down 10% on missed Q1 operating income guidance — the market is currently penalizing the investment cycle, not rewarding it. The 200-day average at $224.69 is close to the 50-day and acting as a combined resistance ceiling. Not attractive at current levels; support at $196.93 is the next watch level, and the signal would upgrade to WATCH only on a stabilization above $210 with the 50-day average flattening.

GOOG.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at CAUTION. Alphabet at $303.21 is 5% below a declining 50-day average of $319.27, with RSI at 42.0 and low volume — weak in every dimension. The advertising and AI business fundamentals remain solid: Q3 2025 revenues grew 16% year-over-year to $102.3 billion (per Marketing Dive), with cloud growth of 47% and search/YouTube ads growing 13.5%. The 7th-generation tensor processing units being used by Meta and Anthropic validate Google's hardware strategy per the Google Cloud Next 2025 keynote, though the Meta chip partnership is noted as unverified in the catalyst data. The problem is purely macro and technical: mega-cap concentration unwind continues, the 50-day average is declining, and the forward price-to-earnings ratio of 22.6x looks less compelling when rate cuts are being pushed out. The 200-day average at $256.15 is more than 15% below current price — a level that would represent a much deeper correction. Not attractive at current levels; would revisit if GOOG reclaims its 50-day average on volume or if macro conditions shift toward rate cuts.

LITE.entry_block: BLOCKED: +22.6% above 50-day SMA (>20% extreme extension). No trend exception applies at this level — entry requires a meaningful pullback regardless of trend duration.
LITE.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at WATCH, but entry is hard-blocked — Lumentum at $616.09 is 22.6% above its 50-day average of $502.35, which triggers the extreme extension block with no exceptions regardless of trend duration or fundamentals. Today's 6.5% decline is notable and may be the beginning of the pullback the setup requires; however, a single-day drop from an extreme extension doesn't constitute a safe entry. The structural catalysts are real: Lumentum was added to the S&P 500 on March 9 per S&P Dow Jones Indices, posted record Q2 FY2026 revenue of $665.5 million (+65.5% year-over-year), and a reported $2 billion investment by NVDA (per CNBC, March 2) — though this specific dollar figure remains unverified pending an SEC filing. At a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 42.8x and enterprise value-to-EBITDA of 172x, valuation is stretched even accounting for the growth trajectory. The nearest meaningful support is $528 (the prior breakout level), and that's where a pullback becomes interesting — roughly 14% below today's close. Add to watchlist if LITE pulls back to the $520-535 range.

COHR.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at WATCH. Coherent at $241.27 is 8.94% above its 50-day average of $221.47 — extended but below the 20% extreme threshold. Signal adjusted from raw WATCH to WATCH (confirmed, entry blocked) — at Step 2 of the extended ticker decision tree, RSI at 49.5 is below 55, but at Step 3, Coherent has been above its 50-day average for 130 sessions (well above the 30-session minimum), and at Step 4 the 50-day average is rising. At Step 5, the driver is structural: S&P 500 inclusion (confirmed per S&P Dow Jones Indices), a reported NVDA investment (per CNBC, unverified pending SEC filing), and AI data center optical interconnect demand represent a multi-quarter demand cycle — this is the one valid trend exception on the watchlist. However, the 8.94% extension is in the 5-20% blocked zone by pipeline, and with broad market conditions deteriorating (SPY at -0.78%, VIX at 27.29), profit-taking risk is elevated. The better entry is a pullback to the $220-225 zone near the rising 50-day average. Resistance at $247.15 is the near-term ceiling; support at $221.47 (50-day) is the key level for any pullback entry.

XLE.entry_block: BLOCKED: +11.4% above 50-day SMA (>5% hard block). Sustained trend exception not met: RSI 70 (>=55).
XLE.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at WATCH, and entry remains hard-blocked — XLE is 11.4% above its 50-day average of $51.65 with RSI at 69.5, which exceeds the 55 threshold required for any trend exception and puts it squarely in overbought territory. The fundamental case is being powered by the Strait of Hormuz disruption: approximately 20% of the world's oil flows through that chokepoint, and with shipping halted, Iraq and Qatar cutting production, per Argus Media and Sprague Energy, energy names are benefiting from supply fear premium. Per AInvest, XLE has outperformed the S&P 500 by 13% in this rotation, with XOM up 4.2% and CVX up 3.8% in Q1. But with crude having already fallen 30% from its $120+ highs on demand destruction fears (per multiple sources), the geopolitical premium is volatile and the entry risk at current levels is high. Today's gain of 0.68% came on low volume — not a conviction move. The entry becomes interesting if XLE pulls back to the $52-54 range (near the 50-day average) on any oil price softening; a stop below $51 would define the risk. Commodity-linked action to watch: if WTI drops back toward $85-88 on demand destruction, XLE may offer a cleaner entry.

PLTR.entry_block: CAUTION: forward P/E of 83x (>40x). Requires explicit justification of why earnings growth supports this multiple — default to WATCH unless thesis is clearly articulated with specific catalysts and timeline.
PLTR.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at HOLD. Palantir at $153.50 is just 0.61% below its 50-day average of $154.45, essentially at the line, with RSI at 56.2 (neutral-bullish) and normal volume. The defense AI thesis is actively strengthening: per CNBC (August 2025), Palantir landed a US Army software and data contract worth up to $10 billion over a decade — importantly, this is an indefinite delivery/indefinite quantity framework, meaning the $10 billion is a ceiling on potential orders rather than guaranteed revenue. Per Rosenblatt analyst notes and Daiwa (cited in the catalyst data), price targets have been raised to $200 and $180 respectively. The 2026 revenue guide of $7.18-7.20 billion (+61% year-over-year) is the underlying earnings growth story that justifies holding despite the 83x forward price-to-earnings ratio — though that multiple remains stretched and the pipeline's soft block requires acknowledging this. The 50-day average at $154.45 is the pivot: a sustained close above it on volume would upgrade this to WATCH; a break below $150 support would trigger reassessment. No new entry until PLTR reclaims the 50-day convincingly.

VUAA_L.signal_rationale: Signal unchanged at CAUTION. The Vanguard S&P 500 UCITS ETF at $129.10 is 2.5% below a declining 50-day average of $132.47, with RSI at 40.1 (neutral-bearish) and low volume — this is essentially a mirror of SPY's deteriorating technical picture. The S&P 500's failure to sustain the March 9 bounce (now in its fourth consecutive session of weakness), the 100-day moving average break, and the VIX spiking to 27.29 (up 12.6% today) collectively confirm the broad index caution. Support at $125.41 is the next level to watch, roughly 2.8% below current price; if that breaks, $118.86 becomes the reference. Not attractive at current levels for a fresh entry — the risk/reward for adding broad index exposure improves significantly if SPY stabilizes above the $125 support zone with VIX declining back below 20.

singapore.summary: The Singapore cluster is uniformly cautious — all five tickers are on CAUTION or HOLD, with the banks (DBS, OCBC, UOB) weighed down by net interest margin compression and C6L's airline squeeze from dual fuel and rerouting costs. The one differentiation worth noting: OCBC's relative Q4 outperformance (YoY profit growth vs. DBS/UOB declines) is not enough to support a WATCH signal while price sits below the 50-day average. Singtel (Z74) remains the only name with structural growth momentum but is blocked by technical extension.
singapore.thesis_status: Broadly bearish — no near-term entry across the cluster
singapore.key_development: Per TTGmice (March 12), Asian carriers including Singapore Airlines are implementing fuel surcharges with longhaul costs potentially tripling, adding operational pressure beyond the oil price impact alone.

semiconductors.summary: The semis cluster has deteriorated sharply today — INTC's 4.8% single-session drop and ASML's breach of the 50-day average pushed both from WATCH to CAUTION, joining AMD which has been there for weeks. NVDA also downgraded to CAUTION. MU holds at WATCH but is blocked ahead of earnings in ~6 days. The mid-cap foundry/memory outperformance thesis that drove INTC and MU to BUY signals just three days ago is being stress-tested by macro headwinds (sticky CPI, rate pause, VIX spike) that are compressing all multiples simultaneously.
semiconductors.key_development: INTC's 4.8% decline without a clear news catalyst is the single most important intraday development in the semis cluster — if this represents institutional selling ahead of earnings, the April 24 print becomes a higher-stakes event than usual.

big_tech.summary: BigTech remains the weakest non-Singapore cluster in terms of entry opportunity — AMZN and GOOG both on CAUTION with declining 50-day averages, and PLTR in a holding pattern just below its 50-day. The cluster-level dynamic is a mega-cap concentration unwind: AMZN's $200B AI capex commitment spooked income-focused investors, and GOOG's 5% gap below the 50-day reflects the same multiple-compression forces hitting all high-growth names as rate cuts are pushed further out.
big_tech.thesis_status: Cautious — no entry across the cluster; PLTR is the only name approaching actionable levels
big_tech.key_development: AMZN and GOOG earnings are both ~42-49 days out — the next meaningful catalyst to reassess the BigTech cluster is those Q1 prints, particularly AWS growth rate and Google Cloud margin trajectory.

photonics.summary: Both LITE and COHR fell sharply today (-6.5% and -2.3% respectively), consistent with profit-taking at extreme technical extensions following the March 9 S&P 500 inclusion spike. LITE's entry block is absolute at 22.6% above the 50-day average; COHR at 8.94% above qualifies for a structural trend exception but the current entry is still too extended. The pullback is directionally healthy for the thesis — the S&P 500 inclusion and reported NVDA investment (per CNBC, unverified) are durable structural catalysts, but require patience.
photonics.thesis_status: Structurally bullish, tactically extended — wait for pullback
photonics.key_development: LITE's 6.5% single-session decline is the largest in the cluster today; if this continues toward the $528 prior breakout level, the WATCH signal will become increasingly actionable.

energy.summary: XLE gained 0.68% today on low volume while the broader market fell — sector rotation into energy continues, but the entry is blocked by RSI at 69.5 and 11.4% extension above the 50-day average. The Hormuz disruption narrative (now Day 14 per the news digest) supports the fundamental case, but the 30% oil price decline from $120+ highs signals demand destruction is taking over from supply fear as the dominant force.
energy.key_development: Per Argus Media (March 11), OPEC+ has kept global supply/demand projections unchanged despite major supply disruptions — the cartel's unwillingness to explicitly address the war signals ongoing price uncertainty that could move either direction.

index.summary: VUAA (the S&P 500 UCITS ETF) mirrors the index's technical breakdown — 2.5% below a declining 50-day average with RSI at 40.1 and VIX at 27.29. Pre-market futures suggesting a modest recovery (+0.39%) provide some relief, but a single session of futures strength after four consecutive sessions of weakness doesn't change the CAUTION stance. The index cluster requires a multi-day sustained move above the 100-day moving average to shift the signal.
index.key_development: VIX spiking 12.6% in a single session to 27.29 is the most important index-level signal today — this level of fear premium suggests institutional hedging activity, not just retail selling, and typically requires VIX to fall back below 22-23 before fresh broad market entries are attractive.

interconnected.name: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (US ADR)
interconnected.reason: NVDA's Blackwell Ultra ramp and MU's high-bandwidth memory capacity expansion both require TSMC advanced node fabrication — a sustained AI data center demand cycle directly translates into TSMC wafer utilisation rates.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if TSM pulls back 8-10% from current levels, bringing it closer to a support zone worth defining; alternatively, monitor after MU's earnings on approximately March 19 for read-through on memory pricing that also affects TSMC's HBM-related capacity loading.

interconnected.name: SK Hynix
interconnected.reason: SK Hynix is the dominant supplier of high-bandwidth memory chips used in NVDA's AI accelerators — MU's earnings on March 19 and the TrendForce 55-60% DRAM price rise forecast provide the same fundamental tailwind to SK Hynix, making it a direct peer check on MU's memory pricing thesis.
interconnected.entry_note: Monitor after MU's earnings on approximately March 19; if MU's guidance confirms the DRAM pricing surge, SK Hynix becomes worth adding to the watchlist — the USD equivalent price would need to pull back at least 7-10% from current levels to represent a fresh entry rather than chasing a move already priced in.

interconnected.name: Arista Networks
interconnected.reason: Arista supplies the high-speed network switching equipment that connects NVDA's AI accelerators inside data centres — hyperscaler capital spending on AI infrastructure (the same buildout driving COHR and LITE's optical interconnect demand) requires Arista's ethernet switching at each rack.
interconnected.entry_note: Add to watchlist if Arista pulls back 10-12% from current levels following any broad tech selloff, particularly if SPY tests the $125 support zone in VUAA that would bring all extended tech names to more attractive entry levels.

interconnected.name: Marvell Technology
interconnected.reason: Marvell designs custom AI accelerator chips (application-specific integrated circuits) for hyperscalers — the same infrastructure buildout narrative driving COHR and LITE's photonics demand also drives demand for Marvell's custom silicon, which requires TSMC advanced nodes and optical interconnects to function.
interconnected.entry_note: Revisit if Marvell pulls back to align with a 10%+ correction from current levels, or after NVDA's next earnings catalyst confirms Blackwell Ultra ramp velocity — Marvell's custom chip revenue is closely correlated with hyperscaler AI commitment timelines.

interconnected.name: Direxion Daily Aerospace & Defense Bull 3X ETF (as a proxy for defense sector) — alternatively, RTX or LMT directly
interconnected.reason: PLTR's defense AI contract wins (US Army $10B IDIQ framework, Navy $448M ShipOS deal) are part of a broader surge in Pentagon AI spending triggered by the Iran conflict — traditional defense contractors like Raytheon (RTX) or Lockheed Martin (LMT) benefit from the same geopolitical catalyst that is driving PLTR's pipeline.
interconnected.entry_note: Monitor RTX or LMT individually rather than a leveraged ETF — add either to the watchlist if the Iran conflict extends beyond 60 days with no resolution, as this would likely trigger additional supplemental defense budget appropriations that create firm (not framework) contract flow.

active_risks: US-Iran military conflict disrupting Strait of Hormuz oil and LNG flows (now Day 14+)
US-China 100% tariff escalation and tech export controls reshaping semiconductor supply chains
Federal Reserve rate pause risk from sticky CPI, compressing tech and growth multiples
geopolitical.macro_outlook: The dominant market transmission mechanism right now is the Hormuz disruption feeding into energy prices, airline costs, and logistics inflation simultaneously — per the Atlantic Council, roughly 20% of global oil flows through the strait, so even partial recovery of traffic would meaningfully reduce the supply premium. The secondary transmission is the Fed: yesterday's CPI print (0.3% monthly rise per the BLS) reinforced per CNBC that rate cut hopes are fading, and with the 10-year Treasury yield at 4.27% and the FOMC meeting on March 19, any hawkish signal could accelerate the multi-week tech multiple compression. US-China tariff escalation (100% tariffs, software export bans per White House statements) adds a third layer of uncertainty specifically for semis with China revenue exposure.

base_case.description: Strait of Hormuz remains partially disrupted but shipping gradually resumes over 4-8 weeks as US-Iran diplomatic back-channels (per Trump's comments suggesting the conflict 'might end soon') produce a limited ceasefire. Oil stabilizes in the $85-100 range as demand destruction balances supply disruption. Fed holds rates at March 19 FOMC and signals patience. Semis rotate based on US domestic supply chain thesis; broad indices remain under pressure but avoid a crash.
base_case.market_impact: SPY range-bound between $660-680. XLE holds gains but doesn't extend significantly. Singapore banks remain under pressure from rate pause. Semis divergence (US domestic vs. China-exposed) continues. VIX gradually declines toward 20-22.
base_case.portfolio_action: Hold current CAUTION stance on broad indices and BigTech. No new broad market (VUAA) exposure until VIX drops below 22. Watch MU earnings on March 19 as the near-term semis inflection point. Singtel (Z74) and COHR remain the closest to actionable entries on any pullback.

optimistic_case.description: Trump-brokered ceasefire announcement within 1-2 weeks reopens Hormuz traffic rapidly. Oil drops back toward $75-80 as supply fears deflate. CPI trend improves sufficiently for Fed to signal one or two cuts before year-end. Tech multiples recover as rate expectations shift dovish.
optimistic_case.market_impact: SPY rally toward $695-710 (reclaiming 100-day moving average). XLE gives back recent gains quickly as oil supply premium collapses. Singapore airlines (C6L) recovers sharply on fuel cost normalization. Tech names (AMZN, GOOG) re-rate as rate expectations improve.
optimistic_case.portfolio_action: If Hormuz de-escalation is confirmed by multiple sources (not just a Trump statement), initiate starter positions in COHR near the 50-day average and watch for Z74 to pull back. C6L/SIA becomes a watch candidate if fuel surcharge narrative reverses.

pessimistic_case.description: Conflict escalates with Iran mining the strait or additional attacks on oil infrastructure. Iraq and Qatar production shutdowns persist beyond 60 days. Oil spikes back toward $115-120. Demand destruction accelerates into recession territory. Fed forced to choose between fighting inflation (oil-driven) and supporting growth.
pessimistic_case.market_impact: SPY breaks below $640 support, testing $620. VIX spikes above 35. XLE initially spikes but then reverses as demand destruction overwhelms supply premium. Airlines (C6L) face existential margin pressure. Singapore banks' non-performing loans tick up on regional economic slowdown.
pessimistic_case.portfolio_action: Pause all new entries across the watchlist. Avoid XLE above current levels — the demand destruction thesis would reverse energy sector gains. Singapore cluster entries pushed out indefinitely. Only defensive position worth considering is GLD (gold) if SPY breaks $640, but note gold is already at historically elevated levels.

wildcard.description: US Supreme Court or Congressional action constrains the Trump administration's ability to maintain 100% China tariffs (Supreme Court already struck down IEEPA tariffs per White House statements), forcing a rapid tariff reversal that dramatically changes the competitive dynamics for US-listed semis with China exposure.
wildcard.market_impact: ASML and AMD would rally sharply on China demand recovery expectations. INTC's US domestic foundry thesis would weaken relative to global peers. Broad tech indices bounce but the move is complex — some names win and some lose depending on China revenue exposure.
wildcard.portfolio_action: If a tariff reversal is confirmed, ASML becomes an immediate WATCH candidate near its 50-day average of $1,363. AMD near its 200-day average of $190 also becomes more interesting. Singapore names and extended positions like LITE and COHR face continued volatility regardless and require patience.

events_this_week.event: CPI Report (February) — Released: Consumer prices rose 0.3% month-over-month per BLS, reinforcing per CNBC that Federal Reserve rate cut hopes are fading and the rate pause narrative is now the base case heading into the March 19 FOMC meeting.
SPY closed at $666.06 (-0.78%), now 4+ sessions below the 100-day moving average — technical breakdown unresolved; next support target around $650-655.
VIX closed at 27.29 (+12.63%) — overnight futures show VIX retreating toward 25.18, but the single-session spike signals institutional hedging activity and argues against new broad market entries.

Strait of Hormuz disruption continues Day 14+ — WTI at approximately $95.76 with data anomaly noted; 5-day trend is the more reliable gauge. Demand destruction vs. supply fear remains the key tension.

PPI Report (February) — Producer Price Index released this morning at 8:30am ET; result not yet incorporated in pipeline data. Watch for any upside surprise that would compound the CPI-driven rate pause narrative.
S&P 500 and Nasdaq futures pointed +0.37-0.39% higher pre-market as of late SGT — expect modestly positive open but elevated volatility given VIX at 27.29 yesterday.

FOMC Rate Decision — Federal Reserve expected to hold rates at 3.50-3.75% (per Kiplinger and US Bank analysis); any hawkish language about inflation persistence could further compress tech multiples. HIGH impact for the entire watchlist.

Micron (MU) Earnings — Approximately 6 days out. Binary event: if management confirms DRAM and NAND pricing surge (TrendForce projects 55-60% DRAM price rise in Q1 2026), MU could re-rate sharply. If guidance disappoints, the WATCH thesis resets. Do NOT enter MU ahead of this print.

ASML Earnings — Approximately 33 days out. Key datapoint: will Q1 2026 order book sustain the Q4 2025 blowout (€13.2B in orders, per TradingView/Invezz)? China tariff impact on order cancellations will be the central question.
Intel (INTC) Earnings — Approximately 42 days out. Today's unexplained 4.8% decline raises the stakes; Q1 2026 foundry progress and CHIPS Act milestones will be closely watched.
Alphabet (GOOG) Earnings — Approximately 42 days out. Key metrics: Google Cloud growth rate, Search revenue resilience, and any update on AI infrastructure capital spending trajectory.
DBS (D05.SI) Earnings — Approximately 48 days out. Net interest margin trajectory and wealth management fee trends will determine whether the CAUTION signal can be revisited.
Amazon (AMZN) Earnings — Approximately 49 days out. AWS revenue acceleration and the pace of $200B capital spending versus operating income recovery will be the key debate.

NVDA Blackwell Ultra (B300) volume production ramp — per NVDA Q4 FY2026 earnings call. If on schedule, this pulls forward the revenue ramp analysts had modeled for later in the year and could re-rate the stock from CAUTION to WATCH/BUY.

AMD MI450 GPU shipments to Meta (reportedly unverified — not confirmed via SEC filing) — if confirmed, validates AMD as a credible NVDA alternative at hyperscale and would likely trigger an upgrade from CAUTION.

portfolio_snapshot.overall_stance: Defensive — most tickers in avoid/wait mode
portfolio_snapshot.risk_posture: The simultaneous VIX spike to 27.29, sticky CPI reinforcing the rate pause, and semis cluster deterioration (INTC -4.8%, ASML and NVDA both downgraded to CAUTION today) have pushed the watchlist to its most defensive posture in this reporting cycle — no BUY signals, 11 CAUTIONs, and the four WATCH names all blocked from entry by technical extension or imminent earnings risk.

new_stocks_to_watch.note: NVDA Blackwell Ultra and MU HBM expansion both run through TSMC advanced nodes — the AI infrastructure build-out directly loads TSMC's fab utilisation. Add to watchlist if TSM pulls back 8-10% from current levels.

Arista's ethernet switching is the connective tissue inside AI data centres — same hyperscaler capex driving COHR and LITE demand also fills Arista's order book. Add to watchlist if Arista pulls back 10-12% from current levels.

Custom AI accelerator chip designer for hyperscalers — Marvell's revenue cycle is correlated with NVDA's Blackwell ramp timeline. Revisit after MU earnings on approximately March 19 for read-through on AI infrastructure commitment pace.
